title: Needle-felted otter
subtitle: A curled sleeping otter in brown and cream merino
excerpt: A curled sleeping otter approximately 12 cm long, built in dark brown merino with a pale cream chest and muzzle. No armature is needed: the solid felted form holds its own shape. Three construction stages cover core, surface colour, and facial details.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from UK guild needle-felted mammal construction methods. Solid-core no-armature approach is standard for small curled animals up to 15 cm. Primary references: guild teaching notes and published needle-felting pattern guides; no single public domain text.

glossaryTerms.definition: Fine-stapled wool from Merino sheep. Felts readily and produces a smooth, dense surface.
Wool fibre drawn into a long, loosely twisted ribbon, ready for spinning or felting.
Combed wool fibres aligned in parallel. Smoother and finer than roving; good for surface detail and colour work.
The tiny notch cut into a felting needle shaft. Barbs catch wool fibres and push them together with each stabbing stroke.

content.text: A sleeping otter is one of the most appealing small mammal subjects for needle felting. The curled posture is forgiving to build: the compact C-shape of the body means you can keep adding and adjusting
merino

until the proportions look right without having to worry about joints or wire. At 12 cm long this project sits in a weekend session; the facial detail at stage three is where you will spend most of your time.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Always stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. Enter foam, not a hard surface, on every stroke.

content.text: Body: 60 g dark brown
merino
roving
. Chest and muzzle: 15 g pale cream merino roving. Detail: small amounts of dark brown and black
wool tops
for the nose and eyes. Optional: two 4 mm black plastic safety eyes.
Method

Stage 1: core body. Take about 40 g of dark brown merino roving and roll it into a sausage approximately 14 cm long. Curve the sausage into a C-shape, bringing the head end and tail end roughly toward each other so the form looks like a curled animal. Place on your foam pad and stab all over with a 38-gauge needle to lock the curve. Work around the entire surface evenly, rotating the form so no one side gets more needle work than another. The aim at this stage is density, not surface finish.

Stage 1 continued: tail. The otter's tail is thick and tapered, roughly one-third the body length. Roll a separate sausage of dark brown roving, about 5 cm long and tapered at one end. Felt it separately until firm, then attach to the rear of the body by felting in along the join. The tail should curve slightly outward from the body, adding to the sleeping pose.

Stage 1 continued: head. Roll a compact ball of dark brown merino, about 4 cm across. The otter's head is rounded and large relative to its body. Felt the ball separately to medium firmness, then press it against the front of the body to check proportion. Once satisfied, felt it in from multiple angles until the join feels solid. Slightly flatten the muzzle area at the front of the head.

Stage 2: cream chest. Take pale cream merino roving and lay thin wisps over the lower half of the head, the chin, and the inner curve of the body where the otter's chest and belly face upward. Felt in carefully with a 38-gauge needle, building up layers until the cream is opaque over the dark base. Keep the boundary between dark and cream soft: blend the two colours slightly at the edges for a natural look.

Stage 2 continued: small ears. Roll two tiny balls of dark brown merino, each about 1 cm across. Felt each into a slight dome shape. Attach one to each side of the top of the head by felting in firmly. Otter ears are small and set far back on the skull; do not place them too far forward or the animal will look like a different species.
Stage 3: facial detail. The muzzle area is the most expressive part. Add a small pad of cream
wool tops

to the front of the muzzle and felt in firmly. The nose is a small black triangle at the very tip: felt in a pinch of black wool tops with a 42-gauge needle, working from the centre outward to get a clean edge. If using safety eyes, push the posts through from outside and press the washers on from behind before the surface colour is too dense to push a needle through cleanly.

Stage 3 continued: surface texture. Use a 42-gauge needle to add a short, textured stroke pattern across the body, simulating the direction of fur growth. On the back, strokes run head-to-tail. On the chest and belly, strokes radiate outward from the centre. This step is optional but adds significant realism.
Finishing: use sharp scissors to trim any stray surface fibres. A very light touch with a lint roller will lift loose fibres without disturbing the form. Check the
barbs
on your needle at this point: fine-detail work on a dense surface dulls needles quickly. Replace if you notice the needle dragging or tearing the surface.

items.symptom: The C-curve keeps straightening out during felting
items.cause: The core was not felted firmly enough before the curve was set
items.fix: Hold the form in the curved position with one hand and felt firmly along the inner curve of the belly with the other, locking the bend with each stroke.

items.symptom: Cream chest shows dark roving bleeding through
items.cause: The cream layer was too thin
items.fix: Add more cream merino in thin wisps, felting each layer in fully before adding the next. Three to four thin layers will cover a dark base cleanly.

items.cause: Wool tops were added too thickly or felted from too many angles
items.fix: Use a single 42-gauge needle and work in from the edges of the nose, pressing each stroke inward toward the centre to get a clean boundary.

items.symptom: Head looks too small or too large
items.cause: The head ball was compared to the core before the tail and surface layers increased the body size
items.fix: Check proportion after all surface layers are added. You can always add more merino to the head to increase it; you cannot easily reduce it, so build conservatively.

content.text: Display and care

The sleeping pose means the otter can rest on any flat surface without mounting. A bed of dried moss or a flat piece of slate makes a natural display base. Keep away from damp conditions: wool felts are not washable once formed. Dust with a very soft brush if the surface collects debris over time.
